[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Bucovethn.png/330px-Bucovethn.png)](/wiki/File:Bucovethn.png)

Ethnic divisions in modern Bukovina with [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians"), [Romanian](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") and [Russian](/wiki/Russians "Russians") areas depicted in light yellow, green, and red respectively. The [Moldovans](/wiki/Moldovans "Moldovans"), counted separately in the [2001 Ukrainian census](/wiki/2001_Ukrainian_census "2001 Ukrainian census"), are included in this map as [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians").

**Bukovina**[[nb 1]](#cite_note-1) is a historical region at the crossroads of [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe").[[1]](#cite_note-2) It is located on the northern slopes of the central [Eastern Carpathians](/wiki/Divisions_of_the_Carpathians "Divisions of the Carpathians") and the adjoining plains, today divided between [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"). It is inhabited mainly by [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") (in southern regions) and [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") (in northern regions).[[2]](#cite_note-brit-3)

Settled throughout history by various cultures and peoples, it became part of the [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'") in the 10th century, but the region was also affected by [Pechenegs](/wiki/Pechenegs "Pechenegs") and [Cumans](/wiki/Cumans "Cumans"). Consequently, the culture of the Kievan Rus' spread in the region, that belonged to the [Principality of Halych](/wiki/Principality_of_Halych "Principality of Halych"). During the time of the [Golden Horde](/wiki/Golden_Horde "Golden Horde"), namely in the 14th century, Bukovina became part of the [Principality of Moldavia](/wiki/Principality_of_Moldavia "Principality of Moldavia"), that was under the suzerainty of the medieval [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"). In that region, the capital of Moldavia, Suceava, was founded.

According to the Moldo-Russian Chronicle, the Hungarian king Vladislav (Ladislaus) asked the Old Romans (i.e. [Byzantines](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire")) and the New Romans (i.e. [Vlachs](/wiki/Vlachs "Vlachs")) to fight the [Tatars](/wiki/Tatars "Tatars"). During the same event, it writes that Dragoș was one of the New Romans. Eventually, Dragoș dismounted Moldavia named from a river ([Moldova River](/wiki/Moldova_River "Moldova River")) flowing in Bukovina. During a Vlach revolt in Bukovina against Balc, Dragoș's grandson, [Bogdan the Founder](/wiki/Bogdan_the_Founder "Bogdan the Founder") joined the revolt and deposed Balc, securing independence from the Kingdom of Hungary. In 1497 a battle took place at the [Cosmin Forest](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Cosmin_Forest "Battle of the Cosmin Forest") (the hilly forests separating [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi") and [Siret](/wiki/Siret "Siret") valleys), at which [Stephen III of Moldavia](/wiki/Stephen_III_of_Moldavia "Stephen III of Moldavia") (Stephen the Great), managed to defeat the much-stronger but demoralized army of King [John I Albert of Poland](/wiki/John_I_Albert_of_Poland "John I Albert of Poland"). The battle is known in Polish popular culture as "the battle when the Knights have perished".

After the Russo-Ottoman [Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca](/wiki/Treaty_of_K%C3%BC%C3%A7%C3%BCk_Kaynarca "Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca") (21 July 1774), the [Habsburg Monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_Monarchy "Habsburg Monarchy") captured the region in October 1774. Under the Austro-Ottoman Convention of [Pera](/wiki/Pera_(Beyo%C4%9Flu) "Pera (Beyoğlu)") (7 May 1775), the region that came to be known as Bukovina was officially ceded to the Habsburgs. From 1775 to 1786 it was organized as a distinctive [Bukovina District](/wiki/Bukovina_District "Bukovina District"), that was included into the [Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria") (1786–1849) and later became the [Duchy of Bukovina](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bukovina "Duchy of Bukovina") (1849–1918).

The first census that recorded ethnicity was made in 1851 and shows a population of 184,718 or 48.5% [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians"), 144,982 or 38.1% [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") and 51,126 or 13.4% others, with a total population of 380,826 people. By 1910, Romanians and Ukrainians were almost in equal numbers with the Romanians concentrated mainly in the south and the Ukrainians mainly in the north. By 1930, following the Kingdom of Romania's acquisition of Bukovina, the region had a total population of 839,500. The region's ethnic composition was approximately 368,500 or 43% Romanian, 235,800 or 28% Ukrainian, 91,100 or 11% [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish"), 75,000 or 9% [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans"), 30,500 or 3.6% [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people"), 12,400 or 1.5% [Hutsul](/wiki/Hutsul "Hutsul"), and 11,800 or 1.4% [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians"), with the remainder consisting of [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians"), [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people"), and other ethnic groups.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

In the summer of 1940, the northern half of Bukovina was [annexed](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina") by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") in violation of the [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact"), a non-aggression pact between [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and the Soviet Union.[[4]](#cite_note-5) On 13 November 1940, [Vyacheslav Molotov](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Molotov "Vyacheslav Molotov") asked [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") to endorse the Soviet annexation of South Bukovina as well. However, by that time, Romania was under a territorial guarantee from the Axis following the [Second Vienna Award](/wiki/Second_Vienna_Award "Second Vienna Award"), so Germany refused. As the *Final Report* of the [Wiesel Commission](/wiki/Wiesel_Commission "Wiesel Commission") put it: "Only Hitler's refusal saved the rest of Bukovina from being swallowed up, Russified, and lost to Romania forever.".[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) Northern Bukovina was temporarily recovered by Romania as an ally of Nazi Germany after the latter invaded the Soviet Union in 1941, but retaken by the Soviet army in 1944.[[2]](#cite_note-brit-3) Bukovina's population was historically ethnically diverse. Today, Bukovina's northern half is the [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast") of Ukraine, while the southern part is [Suceava County](/wiki/Suceava_County "Suceava County") of Romania.[[2]](#cite_note-brit-3) Bukovina is sometimes known as the 'Switzerland of the East', given its diverse ethnic mosaic and deep forested mountainous landscapes.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Duchy_of_Bukovina_within_Austria-Hungary_1914.png/250px-Duchy_of_Bukovina_within_Austria-Hungary_1914.png)](/wiki/File:Duchy_of_Bukovina_within_Austria-Hungary_1914.png)

Map of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") depicting the [Duchy of Bukovina](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bukovina "Duchy of Bukovina"), as part of [Cisleithania](/wiki/Cisleithania "Cisleithania") in 1914.

The name first appears in a document issued by the Voivode of Moldavia [Roman I Mușat](/wiki/Roman_I_of_Moldavia "Roman I of Moldavia") on 30 March 1392, by which he gives to Ionaș Viteazul three villages, located near the [Siret river](/wiki/Siret_(river) "Siret (river)").[[10]](#cite_note-MIac-11)

The name *Bukovina* came into official use in 1775 with the [region's annexation](/wiki/Grigore_III_Ghica "Grigore III Ghica") from the [Principality of Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") to the possessions of the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"), which became the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") in 1804, and [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") in 1867.

The official German name of the province under Austrian rule (1775–1918), *die Bukowina*, was derived from the [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") form *Bukowina*, which in turn was derived from the common [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages") form of *buk*, meaning [beech](/wiki/Beech "Beech") tree (compare [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language") бук [buk]; German *Buche*; [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") *bükkfa*).[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) Another German name for the region, *das Buchenland*, is mostly used in poetry, and means 'beech land', or 'the land of beech trees'. In Romanian, in literary or poetic contexts, the name *Țara Fagilor* ('the land of beech trees') is sometimes used. Often a definite article is used before the name in English too: *the Bukovina*.

In Ukraine, the name Буковина (*Bukovyna*) is unofficial, but is common when referring to the *[Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast")*, as over two-thirds of the [oblast](/wiki/Oblast "Oblast") is the northern part of Bukovina. In Romania, the term *Northern Bukovina* is sometimes synonymous with the entire Chernivtsi Oblast of Ukraine, while *Southern Bukovina* refers to the [Suceava County](/wiki/Suceava_County "Suceava County") of Romania (although 30% of the present-day Suceava County covers territory outside of the historical Bukovina).

The territory of Bukovina had been part of [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'") since the 10th century.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) It then became part of the [Principality of Galicia](/wiki/Principality_of_Galicia "Principality of Galicia"), and then part of [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") in the 14th century. It was first delineated as a separate district of the [Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria") in 1775, and was made a nominal [duchy](/wiki/Duchy "Duchy") within the Austrian Empire in 1849.

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Background")]

Further information: [History of Ukraine](/wiki/History_of_Ukraine "History of Ukraine"), [Antes (people)](/wiki/Antes_(people) "Antes (people)"), [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia"), [Romania in the Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Romania_in_the_Early_Middle_Ages "Romania in the Early Middle Ages"), and [Origin of the Romanians](/wiki/Origin_of_the_Romanians "Origin of the Romanians")

The region, which is made up of a portion of the northeastern Carpathian Mountains and the neighbouring plain, was settled by both [Vlachs](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") and [Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians"). After being inhabited by ancient peoples and tribes ([Trypillian](/wiki/Trypillian_culture "Trypillian culture"), Scythians, Dacians, Getae) starting from the Paleolithic, Germanic culture and language emerged in the region in the 4th century by the time of the Goths, archeological research has also indicated that the Romans had a presence in the region. Later, Slavic culture spread, and by the 10th century the region was part of Turkic, Slavic and Romance people like Pechenegs, Cumans, Ruthinians and Vlachs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Among the first references of the [Vlachs](/wiki/Vlachs "Vlachs") (Romanians) in the region is in the 10th century by Varangian Sagas referring to the Blakumen people i.e. Vlachs in the land of Pechenegs. By late 12th century chronicle of [Niketas Choniates](/wiki/Niketas_Choniates "Niketas Choniates"), writes that some Vlachs seized the future Byzantine emperor, [Andronikos Komnenos](/wiki/Andronikos_I_Komnenos "Andronikos I Komnenos"), when "he reached the borders of [Halych](/wiki/Principality_of_Halych "Principality of Halych")" in 1164. In the Moldo-Russian Chronicle, writes the events of year 1342, that the Hungarian king Vladislav (Ladislaus) asked the Old Romans and the New Romans to fight the Tatars, by that they will earn a sit in Maramureș. During the same event, it writes that Dragoș was one of the Romans .[[14]](#cite_note-15) In the year 1359 Dragoș dismounted Moldavia and took with him many Vlachs and German colonists from Maramureș to Moldavia.[[2]](#cite_note-brit-3)[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

### Early settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early settlement")]

First traces of human occupation date back to the Paleolithic.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14) The area was first settled by [Trypillian culture](/wiki/Trypillian_culture "Trypillian culture") tribes, in the Neolithic. It was then settled by now extinct tribes ([Dacians](/wiki/Dacians "Dacians")/[Getae](/wiki/Getae "Getae"), [Thracian](/wiki/Thracian "Thracian")/[Scythian](/wiki/Scythians "Scythians") tribes). Meanwhile, many nomads crossed the region (3rd to 9th century A.D). By the 4th century, the Goths appeared in the region.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) And later by the 5th and 6th century Slavic people appeared in the region. They were part of the tribal alliance of the [Antes](/wiki/Antes_(people) "Antes (people)"). In the 9th century [Tivertsi](/wiki/Tivertsi "Tivertsi") and [White Croatians](/wiki/White_Croatia "White Croatia") and [Cowari](/w/index.php?title=Cowari&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cowari (page does not exist)") composed the local population.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

### Kievan Rus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Kievan Rus")]

According to the Russian and Ukrainian historians, Bukovina is an "ancient Slavic land".[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19)

United by [Prince Oleg](/wiki/Oleg_the_Wise "Oleg the Wise") in the 870s, Kievan Rus' was a loose federation of speakers of [East Slavic](/wiki/East_Slavic_languages "East Slavic languages") and [Uralic languages](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") from the late 9th to the mid-13th century,[[19]](#cite_note-channon-20)[[20]](#cite_note-Kievan-21) under the reign of the [Rurik dynasty](/wiki/Rurik_dynasty "Rurik dynasty"), founded by the [Varangian](/wiki/Varangians "Varangians") prince [Rurik](/wiki/Rurik "Rurik").[[20]](#cite_note-Kievan-21) Bukovina gradually became part of Kievan Rus' from the late 10th century and Pechenegs[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Bukovina#Nonsensical._"from_the_late_10th_century_and_Pechenegs"_mixes_a_time_unit_with_an_ethnic_group. "Talk:Bukovina")*].[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) Parts of Bukovina were first conquered in 981 by [Vladimir the Great](/wiki/Vladimir_the_Great "Vladimir the Great"). The rest was incorporated into the [Principality of Terebovlia](/wiki/Principality_of_Terebovlia "Principality of Terebovlia") in 1084. When Kievan Rus' was partitioned at the end of the 11th century, Bukovina became part of the [Principality of Galicia-Volhynia](/wiki/Principality_of_Galicia-Volhynia "Principality of Galicia-Volhynia").[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

### Principality of Galicia–Volhynia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Principality of Galicia–Volhynia")]

After the fragmentation of Kievan Rus', Bukovina passed to the Principality of Galicia ([Principality of Galicia-Volhynia](/wiki/Principality_of_Galicia-Volhynia "Principality of Galicia-Volhynia")) in 1124. The Church in Bukovina was initially administered from [Kiev](/wiki/Metropolis_of_Kiev_and_all_Rus%27 "Metropolis of Kiev and all Rus'"). In 1302, it was passed to the [Halych metropoly](/wiki/Metropolis_of_Halych "Metropolis of Halych").[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

After the Mongols under Batu invaded Europe, with the region nominally [falling into their hands](/wiki/Batu%27s_raid_of_1240_in_Ruthenia "Batu's raid of 1240 in Ruthenia"), ties between Galician-Volhynian and Bukovina weakened. As a result of the Mongol invasion, the [Shypyntsi land](/w/index.php?title=Shypyntsi_land&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shypyntsi land (page does not exist)"), recognizing the suzerainty of the Mongols, arose in the region.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

Eventually, this state collapsed, and Bukovina passed to Hungary. [King Louis I](/wiki/Louis_I_of_Hungary "Louis I of Hungary") appointed [Dragoș, Voivode of Moldavia](/wiki/Drago%C8%99,_Voivode_of_Moldavia "Dragoș, Voivode of Moldavia") as his deputy, facilitating the migration of the Romanians from [Maramureș](/wiki/Maramure%C8%99 "Maramureș") and [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania").[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

The [Moldavian state](/wiki/Principality_of_Moldavia "Principality of Moldavia") was formed by the mid-14th century, eventually expanding its territory all the way to the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"). Upon its foundation, the Moldavian or Moldovan state recognized the supremacy of Poland, keeping on recognizing it from 1387 to 1497.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14) Later (1514) it was vassalized by the Ottoman Empire.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14) Bukovina and neighboring regions became the nucleus of the Moldavian Principality, with the capital successively moving from [Baia](/wiki/Baia "Baia"), to [Siret](/wiki/Siret "Siret"), [Suceava](/wiki/Suceava "Suceava"), and finally in 1564 to [Iași](/wiki/Ia%C8%99i "Iași"), where it would remain until the end of the principality's history. The name of [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") ([Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Moldova*) is derived from the [Moldova River](/wiki/Moldova_River "Moldova River") in Bukovina.

### Polish and Moldavian period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Polish and Moldavian period")]

[Petru II](/wiki/Petru_II_of_Moldavia "Petru II of Moldavia") moved the seat of Moldova from [Siret](/wiki/Siret "Siret") to [Suceava](/wiki/Suceava "Suceava") in 1388. In the 15th century, [Pokuttya](/wiki/Pokuttya "Pokuttya"), the region immediately to the north, became the subject of disputes between the Principality of Moldavia and the [Polish Kingdom](/wiki/History_of_Poland_(1385%E2%80%931569) "History of Poland (1385–1569)"). Pokuttya was inhabited by [Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians") (the predecessors of modern Ukrainians together with the [Rus'](/wiki/Rus%27_people "Rus' people"), and of the [Rusyns](/wiki/Rusyns "Rusyns")). In 1497 a battle took place at the [Cosmin Forest](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Cosmin_Forest "Battle of the Cosmin Forest") (the hilly forests separating [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi") and [Siret](/wiki/Siret "Siret") valleys), at which [Stephen III of Moldavia](/wiki/Stephen_III_of_Moldavia "Stephen III of Moldavia") (Stephen the Great), managed to defeat the much-stronger but demoralized army of King [John I Albert of Poland](/wiki/John_I_Albert_of_Poland "John I Albert of Poland"). The battle is known in Polish popular culture as "the battle when the Knights have perished". The region had been under Polish nominal suzerainty from its foundation (1387) to the time of this battle (1497). Shortly thereafter, it became a vassal of the Ottoman Empire (1514).[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Cetatea_de_Scaun_a_Sucevei_la_ceas_de_seara.jpg/250px-Cetatea_de_Scaun_a_Sucevei_la_ceas_de_seara.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cetatea_de_Scaun_a_Sucevei_la_ceas_de_seara.jpg)

View over the western side of the [medieval Seat Fortress of Suceava](/wiki/Medieval_Seat_Fortress_of_Suceava "Medieval Seat Fortress of Suceava").

In this period, the patronage of Stephen the Great and his successors on the throne of Moldavia saw the construction of the famous painted monasteries of [Moldovița](/wiki/Moldovi%C8%9Ba_Monastery "Moldovița Monastery"), [Sucevița](/wiki/Sucevi%C8%9Ba_Monastery "Sucevița Monastery"), [Putna](/wiki/Putna_Monastery "Putna Monastery"), [Humor](/wiki/Humor_Monastery "Humor Monastery"), [Voroneț](/wiki/Vorone%C8%9B "Voroneț"), [Dragomirna](/wiki/Dragomirna_Monastery "Dragomirna Monastery"), [Arbore](/wiki/Arbore_Church "Arbore Church") and others. With their renowned exterior [frescoes](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco"), these monasteries remain some of the greatest cultural treasures of Romania; some of them are [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), part of the [painted churches of northern Moldavia](/wiki/Painted_churches_of_northern_Moldavia "Painted churches of northern Moldavia"). The most famous monasteries are in the area of [Suceava](/wiki/Suceava "Suceava"), which today is part of Romania. Also part of Romania is the monastery of [John the New](/wiki/John_the_New "John the New") [[ro](https://ro.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sf%C3%A2ntul_Ioan_cel_Nou "ro:Sfântul Ioan cel Nou"); [uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%99%D0%BE%D0%B0%D0%BD_%D0%9D%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B9_(%D0%A1%D1%83%D1%87%D0%B0%D0%B2%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9) "uk:Йоан Новий (Сучавський)")], an Orthodox saint and martyr, who was killed by the Tatars in [Bilhorod-Dnistrovskyi](/wiki/Bilhorod-Dnistrovskyi "Bilhorod-Dnistrovskyi").

From 1490 to 1492, the Mukha rebellion, led by the Ukrainian hero [Petro Mukha](/wiki/Petro_Mukha "Petro Mukha"), took place in Galicia.[[21]](#cite_note-mukha-22) This event pitted the Moldavians against the oppressive rule of the Polish magnates. A rebel army composed of Moldavian peasants took the fortified towns of Sniatyn, Kolomyia, and Halych, killing many Polish noblemen and burghers, before being halted by the Polish Royal Army in alliance with a Galician *[levée en masse](/wiki/Lev%C3%A9e_en_masse "Levée en masse")* and Prussian mercenaries while marching to Lviv. Many rebels died in the Rohatyn Battle, with Mukha and the survivors fleeing back to Moldavia. Mukha returned to Galicia to re-ignite the rebellion, but was killed in 1492.[[21]](#cite_note-mukha-22)

In May 1600 [Mihai Viteazul (Michael the Brave)](/wiki/Michael_the_Brave "Michael the Brave"), became the ruler the two Danubian principalities and Transylvania.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

In the 16th and 17th centuries, Ukrainian warriors (*[Cossacks](/wiki/Ukrainian_Cossacks "Ukrainian Cossacks")*) were involved in many conflicts against the Turkish and Tatar invaders of the Moldavian territory. Notably, [Ivan Pidkova](/wiki/Ivan_Pidkova "Ivan Pidkova"), best known as the subject of Ukraine's bard [Taras Shevchenko](/wiki/Taras_Shevchenko "Taras Shevchenko")'s *Ivan Pidkova* (1840), led military campaigns in the 1570s.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14) Many Bukovinians joined the Cossacks during the [Khmelnytsky uprising](/wiki/Khmelnytsky_uprising "Khmelnytsky uprising"). As part of the peasant armies, they formed their own regiment, which participated to the 1648 siege of Lviv. Ukrainian [Hetman](/wiki/Hetman "Hetman") [Bohdan Khmelnytsky](/wiki/Bohdan_Khmelnytsky "Bohdan Khmelnytsky") himself led a campaign in Moldavia, whose result was an alliance between Khmelnytsky and its *hospodar* [Vasile Lupu](/wiki/Vasile_Lupu "Vasile Lupu").[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14) Other prominent Ukrainian leaders fighting against the Turks in Moldovia were [Severyn Nalyvaiko](/wiki/Severyn_Nalyvaiko "Severyn Nalyvaiko") and [Petro Konashevych-Sahaidachny](/wiki/Petro_Konashevych-Sahaidachny "Petro Konashevych-Sahaidachny").[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Monumentul_lui_Grigore_Ghica_III_din_Ia%C5%9Fi7.jpg/250px-Monumentul_lui_Grigore_Ghica_III_din_Ia%C5%9Fi7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monumentul_lui_Grigore_Ghica_III_din_Ia%C5%9Fi7.jpg)

Monument in [Iași](/wiki/Ia%C8%99i "Iași") (1875) dedicated to [Grigore III Ghica](/wiki/Grigore_III_Ghica "Grigore III Ghica") and Moldavia's loss of Bukovina.

For short periods of time (during wars), the Polish Kingdom (to which Moldavians were hostile) again occupied parts of northern Moldavia. However, the old border was re-established each time, as for example on 14 October 1703 the Polish delegate Martin Chometowski said, according to the Polish protocol, "Between us and [Wallachia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") (i.e. the Moldavian region, vassal of the Turks) God himself set [Dniester](/wiki/Dniester "Dniester") as the border" (*Inter nos et Valachiam ipse Deus flumine Tyras dislimitavit*). According to the Turkish protocol the sentence reads, "God (may He be exalted) has separated the lands of Moldavia [Bukovina, vassal of the Turks] from our Polish lands by the river Dniester." Strikingly similar sentences were used in other sayings and folkloristic anecdotes, such as the phrase reportedly exclaimed by a member of the Aragonese Cortes in 1684.[[23]](#cite_note-Ottoman-24)

### Habsburg Bukovina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Habsburg Bukovina")]

Main articles: [Bukovina District](/wiki/Bukovina_District "Bukovina District"), [Duchy of Bukovina](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bukovina "Duchy of Bukovina"), and [Early Modern Romania](/wiki/Early_Modern_Romania "Early Modern Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Palace_of_Justice%2C_Vienna_-_Aula%2C_Coat_of_Arms_-_Herzogtum_Bukowina_4216.jpg/250px-Palace_of_Justice%2C_Vienna_-_Aula%2C_Coat_of_Arms_-_Herzogtum_Bukowina_4216.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palace_of_Justice,_Vienna_-_Aula,_Coat_of_Arms_-_Herzogtum_Bukowina_4216.jpg)

The coat of arms of Bukovina, a constituent country of the [Imperial Austrian Council](/wiki/Imperial_Council_(Austria) "Imperial Council (Austria)"), depicted at the Assembly Hall in the [Viennese Justice Palace](/wiki/Palace_of_Justice,_Vienna "Palace of Justice, Vienna").

During the Russo-Turkish War (1768–1774), the Ottoman armies were defeated by the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), and the Principality of Moldavia was temporarily possessed by Russian forces, from the autumn 1769 to September 1774. Already before the [Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca](/wiki/Treaty_of_K%C3%BC%C3%A7%C3%BCk_Kaynarca "Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca") (21 July 1774), the non-belligerent [Habsburg Monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_Monarchy "Habsburg Monarchy") was already planning to occupy north-western regions of Moldavia, claiming the need for *a road between Galicia and Transylvania* and thus, while Russian armies were retreating upon the conclusion of the peace treaty, Habsburg forces entered and captured the region in October 1774. The Ottomans protested, but consequently, in order to avoid another war, agreed to relinquish the territory. Under the Austro-Ottoman Convention of [Pera](/wiki/Pera_(Beyo%C4%9Flu) "Pera (Beyoğlu)") (7 May 1775), the region that came to be known as Bukovina was officially ceded to the Habsburgs.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Prince [Grigore III Ghica](/wiki/Grigore_III_Ghica "Grigore III Ghica") of [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") protested and was prepared to take action to recover the territory, but was assassinated, and a Greek-[Phanariot](/wiki/Phanariotes "Phanariotes") foreigner was put on the throne of [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") by the Ottomans.

On 2 July 1776, at Palamutka, Austrians and Ottomans signed a border convention, Austria giving back 59 of the previously occupied villages, retaining 278 villages.

Bukovina was a closed military district (1775–1786), then the largest district, [Bukovina District](/wiki/Bukovina_District "Bukovina District") (first known as the Czernowitz District), of the Austrian constituent [Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria") (1787–1849). On 4 March 1849, Bukovina became a separate Austrian [Kronland](/wiki/Crown_land#Austria "Crown land") 'crown land' under a *[Landespräsident](/w/index.php?title=Landespr%C3%A4sident&action=edit&redlink=1 "Landespräsident (page does not exist)")* (not a *Statthalter*, as in other crown lands) and was declared the [Duchy of Bukovina](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bukovina "Duchy of Bukovina") *Herzogtum Bukowina* (a nominal duchy, as part of the official full style of the Austrian Emperors). In 1860 it was again amalgamated with Galicia but reinstated as a separate province once again on 26 February 1861, a status that would last until 1918.[[25]](#cite_note-hist-26)

In 1849 Bukovina got a representative assembly, the *Landtag* ([diet](/wiki/Diet_(assembly) "Diet (assembly)")). The [Moldavian](/wiki/Moldovans "Moldovans") nobility had traditionally formed the ruling class in that territory. In 1867, with the re-organization of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") as the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), it became part of the [Cisleithanian](/wiki/Cisleithania "Cisleithania") or Austrian territories of Austria-Hungary and remained so until 1918.

#### Late 19th to early 20th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Late 19th to early 20th centuries")]

Main articles: [Early Modern Romania](/wiki/Early_Modern_Romania "Early Modern Romania") and [History of Ukraine](/wiki/History_of_Ukraine "History of Ukraine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Bucovina.JPG/250px-Bucovina.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bucovina.JPG)

Map of the Austrian crownland of Bukovina at the turn of the 20th century.

The 1871 and 1904 celebrations held at [Putna Monastery](/wiki/Putna_Monastery "Putna Monastery"), near the tomb of [Stephen the Great](/wiki/Stephen_III_of_Moldavia "Stephen III of Moldavia"), constituted tremendous moments for Romanian national identity in Bukovina. Since gaining its independence, the [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania") had had designs on incorporating this province into its new Kingdom. Romanians considered it to be a core part of the old Principality of Moldavia, and of great significance to its [history](/wiki/History_of_Romania "History of Romania"). It contained many prominent historical Moldavian monuments, [art](/wiki/Art_of_Romania "Art of Romania") and architecture and remained a strong cultural anchor for Moldavians in particular.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

During the Habsburg period, the Ukrainian population increased in the north of the region, while in the south the ethnic Romanian population remained the majority population. The Austrians "managed to keep a balance between the various ethnic groups."[[2]](#cite_note-brit-3) In the 1880 census, there were 239,690 Ruthenians and [Hutzuls](/wiki/Hutzul "Hutzul"), or roughly 41.5% of the regions population, while Romanians were second with 190,005 people or 33%, a ratio that remained more or less the same until [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). The percentage of Romanians fell from 85.3% in 1774[[27]](#cite_note-roman1774-28)[[28]](#cite_note-ceeol-29) to 34.1% in 1910.[[10]](#cite_note-MIac-11) Ruthenians is an archaic name for [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians"), while the [Hutsuls](/wiki/Hutsul "Hutsul") are a regional Ukrainian subgroup.

### Ukrainian national sentiment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ukrainian national sentiment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/%D0%A1%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BD_%D0%A1%D0%BC%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C-%D0%A1%D1%82%D0%BE%D1%86%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9.png/250px-%D0%A1%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BD_%D0%A1%D0%BC%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C-%D0%A1%D1%82%D0%BE%D1%86%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9.png)](/wiki/File:%D0%A1%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BD_%D0%A1%D0%BC%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C-%D0%A1%D1%82%D0%BE%D1%86%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9.png)

[Stepan Smal-Stotsky](/wiki/Stepan_Smal-Stotsky "Stepan Smal-Stotsky"), 1893

Ukrainian national sentiment re-ignited in the 1840s. Officially started in 1848, the nationalist movement gained strength in 1869, when the [Ruska Besida Society](/w/index.php?title=Ruska_Besida_Society&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ruska Besida Society (page does not exist)") was founded in [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi"). By the 1890s, Ukrainians were represented in the regional diet and Vienna parliament, being led by [Stepan Smal-Stotsky](/wiki/Stepan_Smal-Stotsky "Stepan Smal-Stotsky"). Beside Stotsky, other important Bukovinian leaders were [Yerotei Pihuliak](/w/index.php?title=Yerotei_Pihuliak&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yerotei Pihuliak (page does not exist)"), [Omelian Popovych](/w/index.php?title=Omelian_Popovych&action=edit&redlink=1 "Omelian Popovych (page does not exist)"), [Mykola Vasylko](/w/index.php?title=Mykola_Vasylko&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mykola Vasylko (page does not exist)"), [Orest Zybachynsky](/w/index.php?title=Orest_Zybachynsky&action=edit&redlink=1 "Orest Zybachynsky (page does not exist)") [[uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%97%D0%B8%D0%B1%D0%B0%D1%87%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%9E%D1%80%D0%B5%D1%81%D1%82_%D0%A0%D1%83%D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%84%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87 "uk:Зибачинський Орест Рудольфович")], [Denys Kvitkovsky](/w/index.php?title=Denys_Kvitkovsky&action=edit&redlink=1 "Denys Kvitkovsky (page does not exist)")  [[uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%9A%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%82%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%94%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%B8%D1%81_%D0%92%D0%B0%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87 "uk:Квітковський Денис Васильович")], Sylvester Nikorovych, Ivan and Petro Hryhorovych, and Lubomyr Husar.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) The first periodical in the Ukrainian language, *Bukovyna* (published from 1885 until 1918) was published by the populists since the 1880s. The Ukrainian populists fought for their ethnocultural rights against the Austrians.

Peasant revolts broke out in Hutsul areas in the 1840s, with the peasants demanding more rights, socially and politically. Likewise, nationalist sentiment spread among the Romanians. As a result, more rights were given to Ukrainians and Romanians, with five Ukrainians (including notably [Lukian Kobylytsia](/wiki/Lukjan_Kobylytsia "Lukjan Kobylytsia")), two Romanians and one German elected to represent the region.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) The Ukrainians won representation at the provincial diet as late as 1890, and fought for equality with the Romanians also in the religious sphere. This was partly achieved only as late as on the eve of World War I.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) However, their achievements were accompanied by friction with Romanians. Overpopulation in the countryside caused migration (especially to North America), also leading to peasant strikes. However, by 1914 Bukovina managed to get "the best Ukrainian schools and cultural-educational institutions of all the regions of Ukraine."[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) Beside Ukrainians, also [Bukovina's Germans](/wiki/Bukovina_Germans "Bukovina Germans") and Jews, as well as a number of Romanians and Hungarians, emigrated in 19th and 20th century.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Bukovina1910ethnic.jpg/250px-Bukovina1910ethnic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bukovina1910ethnic.jpg)

Ethnic groups in Bukovina 1775–1930 (Ukrainians in red, Romanians in green).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Chernivtsi_-_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96_1.jpg/250px-Chernivtsi_-_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chernivtsi_-_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96_1.jpg)

Czernowitz c. 1905

Under Austrian rule, Bukovina remained ethnically mixed: [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") were predominant in the south, [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") (commonly referred to as [Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians") in the Empire) in the north, with small numbers of Hungarian [Székelys](/wiki/Sz%C3%A9kelys "Székelys"), [Slovak](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks"), and Polish peasants, and [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans"), [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") and Jews in the towns. The 1910 census counted 800,198 people, of which: [Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians") 38.88%, [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") 34.38%, [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans") 21.24% (Jews 12.86% included), [Polish people](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") 4.55%, [Hungarian people](/wiki/Hungarian_people "Hungarian people") 1.31%, [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks "Slovaks") 0.08%, [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") 0.02%, [Italian people](/wiki/Italian_people "Italian people") 0.02%, and a few [Croats](/wiki/Croat "Croat"), [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people"), [Serbs](/wiki/Serbs "Serbs") and [Turkish people](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people"). While reading the statistics it should be mentioned that, due to "adverse economic conditions", some 50,000 Ukrainians left the region (mostly emigrating to North America) between 1891 and 1910, in the aforementioned migrations.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14) Nonetheless, the percentage of Ukrainians has significantly grown since the end of the eighteenth century.[[10]](#cite_note-MIac-11)

In 1783, by an [imperial decree](/wiki/Decree "Decree") of [Joseph II](/wiki/Joseph_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor"), the local [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") Eparchy of Bukovina (with its seat in [Czernowitz](/wiki/Czernowitz "Czernowitz")) was placed under spiritual jurisdiction of the [Metropolitanate of Karlovci](/wiki/Metropolitanate_of_Karlovci "Metropolitanate of Karlovci").[[32]](#cite_note-33) Some friction appeared in time between the church hierarchy and the Romanians, complaining that [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic") was favored to [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), and that family names were being [slavicized](/wiki/Slavicisation "Slavicisation").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In spite of Romanian-Slavic speaking frictions over the influence in the local church hierarchy, there was no Romanian-Ukrainian inter-ethnic tension, and both cultures developed in educational and public life. After the rise of Ukrainian nationalism in 1848[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14) and the following rise of Romanian nationalism, Habsburg authorities reportedly awarded additional rights to Ukrainians in an attempt to temper Romanian ambitions of independence.[[33]](#cite_note-34) On the other hand, the Ukrainians had to struggle against the Austrians, with the Austrians rejecting both nationalist claims, favoring neither Romanians nor Ukrainians, while attempting to "keep a balance between the various ethnic groups."[[2]](#cite_note-brit-3)[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) Indeed, a group of scholars surrounding the [Austrian Archduke Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Archduke_Franz_Ferdinand_of_Austria "Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria") were planning to turn Austria-Hungary into a [federation](/wiki/United_States_of_Greater_Austria "United States of Greater Austria"). These plans included creating a majority-Romanian state of *Transylvania* within the federation which would have included Bukovina with Czernowitz.[[34]](#cite_note-abc-35)[[35]](#cite_note-buko-36)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] After they acquired Bukovina, the Austrians opened only one elementary school in Czernowitz (Ukrainian 'Chernivsti', Romanian 'Cernăuți), which taught exclusively in Romanian. They later did open German schools, but no Ukrainian ones. Ukrainian language would appear in Chernivsti's schools as late as 1851, but only as a subject, at the local university (in spite of this, the city attracted students from other parts of Bukovina and Galicia, who would study in the German language of instruction).[[36]](#cite_note-chern-37) [Lukjan Kobylytsia](/wiki/Lukjan_Kobylytsia "Lukjan Kobylytsia"), a Ukrainian Bukovinian farmer and activist, died of torture-related causes after attempting to ask for more rights for the Bukovinian Ukrainians to the Austrians. He died of the consequence of torture in 1851 in Romania. At the end of the 19th century, the development of [Ukrainian culture](/wiki/Ukrainian_culture "Ukrainian culture") in Bukovina surpassed [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Central_Europe) "Galicia (Central Europe)") and the rest of Ukraine with a network of Ukrainian educational facilities, while [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia") formed an archbishopric, later raised to the rank of *Metropolitanate*.

In 1873, the Eastern Orthodox Bishop of [Czernowitz](/wiki/Czernowitz "Czernowitz") (who was since 1783 under the spiritual jurisdiction of the Metropolitan of Karlovci) was elevated to the rank of Archbishop, when a new [Metropolitanate of Bukovinian and Dalmatia](/wiki/Residence_of_Bukovinian_and_Dalmatian_Metropolitans "Residence of Bukovinian and Dalmatian Metropolitans") was created. The new archbishop of [Czernowitz](/wiki/Czernowitz "Czernowitz") gained supreme jurisdiction in all [Cisleithania](/wiki/Cisleithania "Cisleithania"), over "Serbian" eparchies of [Dalmatia](/wiki/Eparchy_of_Dalmatia "Eparchy of Dalmatia") and [Kotor](/wiki/Kotor "Kotor"), which were also (until then) under the spiritual jurisdiction of the [Patriarchate of Karlovci](/wiki/Patriarchate_of_Karlovci "Patriarchate of Karlovci").

In the early 20th century, a group of scholars surrounding the Austrian [Archduke Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Archduke_Franz_Ferdinand "Archduke Franz Ferdinand") created a plan (that never came to pass) of [United States of Greater Austria](/wiki/United_States_of_Greater_Austria "United States of Greater Austria"). The specific proposal was published in Aurel C. Popovici's book "Die Vereinigten Staaten von Groß-Österreich" [The United States of Greater Austria], Leipzig, 1906. According to it, most of Bukovina (including Czernowitz) would form, with [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), a Romanian state, while the north-western portion (Zastavna, Kozman, Waschkoutz, Wiznitz, Gura Putilei, and Seletin districts) would form with the bigger part of [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Central_Europe) "Galicia (Central Europe)") a Ukrainian state, both in a federation with 13 other states under the Austrian crown.[[34]](#cite_note-abc-35)[[35]](#cite_note-buko-36)

### Kingdom of Romania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Kingdom of Romania")]

Main articles: [Union of Bukovina with Romania](/wiki/Union_of_Bukovina_with_Romania "Union of Bukovina with Romania") and [Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania")

In [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), several battles were fought in Bukovina between the [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), [German](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"), and [Russian](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia") armies, which resulted in the Russian army invading Chernivtsi for three times (30 August to 21 October 1914, 26 November 1914 to 18 February 1915 and 18 June 1916 to 2 August 1917). The regime that had occupied the city pursued a policy of persecution of "nationally conscious Ukrainians". The situation was not improved until the [February Revolution of 1917](/wiki/February_Revolution_of_1917 "February Revolution of 1917").[[36]](#cite_note-chern-37) The Russian were driven out in 1917. Bukovina suffered great losses during the war.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

With the collapse of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") in 1918, both the local [Romanian National Council](/wiki/Romanian_National_Council "Romanian National Council") and the [Ukrainian National Council](/wiki/Ukrainian_National_Council "Ukrainian National Council") based in Galicia claimed the region. In the beginning, Bukovina joined the fledging [West Ukrainian National Republic](/wiki/West_Ukrainian_National_Republic "West Ukrainian National Republic") (November 1918), but it was occupied by the Romanian army immediately thereafter.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)

A Constituent Assembly on 14/27 October 1918 formed an executive committee, to whom the Austrian governor of the province handed power. After an official request by [Iancu Flondor](/wiki/Iancu_Flondor "Iancu Flondor"), Romanian troops swiftly moved in to take over the territory, against Ukrainian protest.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) Although local Ukrainians attempted to incorporate parts of Northern Bukovina into the short-lived [West Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/West_Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "West Ukrainian People's Republic"), this attempt was defeated by Polish and Romanian troops.

The Ukrainian Regional Committee, led by Omelian Popovych, organized a rally in Chernivtsi on 3 November 1918, demanding Bukovina's annexation to Ukraine. The committee took power in the Ukrainian part of Bukovina, including its biggest center Chernivtsi.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) The Romanian moderates, who were led by [Aurel Onciul](/wiki/Aurel_Onciul "Aurel Onciul"), accepted the division. However, the Romanian conservatives, led by [Iancu Flondor](/wiki/Iancu_Flondor "Iancu Flondor"), rejected the idea. In spite of Ukrainian resistance, the Romanian army occupied the Northern Bukovina, including Chernivtsi, on 11 November.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

Under the protection of Romanian troops, the Romanian Council summoned a [General Congress of Bukovina](/wiki/General_Congress_of_Bukovina "General Congress of Bukovina") for 15/28 November 1918, where 74 Romanians, 13 Ruthenians, 7 Germans, and 6 Poles were represented (this is the linguistic composition, and Jews were not recorded as a separate group).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] According to Romanian historiography, popular enthusiasm swept the whole region, and a large number of people gathered in the city to wait for the resolution of the Congress.[[37]](#cite_note-CK-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39) The council was quickly summoned by the Romanians upon their occupation of Bukovina.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Interbelic_Suceava_County_CoA.png/250px-Interbelic_Suceava_County_CoA.png)](/wiki/File:Interbelic_Suceava_County_CoA.png)

Coat of arms of interwar Suceava county in the Kingdom of Romania

The Congress elected the Romanian Bukovinian politician Iancu Flondor as chairman, and voted for the union with the [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"), with the support of the Romanian, German, and Polish representatives; the Ukrainians did not support this.[[39]](#cite_note-rezumat-40) The reasons stated were that, until its takeover by the Habsburg in 1775, Bukovina was the heart of the [Principality of Moldavia](/wiki/Principality_of_Moldavia "Principality of Moldavia"), where the *gropnițele domnești* (voivods' burial sites) are located, and *dreptul de liberă hotărâre de sine* (right of self-determination).[[nb 2]](#cite_note-41) Romanian control of the province was recognized internationally in the [Treaty of St. Germain](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1919) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1919)") in 1919. Bukovina's autonomy was undone during Romanian occupation, the region being reduced to an ordinary Romanian province.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14) It was subject to martial law from 1918 to 1928, and again from 1937 to 1940.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)

The Ukrainian language was suppressed, "educational and cultural institutions, newspapers and magazines were closed."[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)

Romanian authorities oversaw a renewed programme of [Romanianization](/wiki/Romanianization "Romanianization") aiming its assimilationist policies at the Ukrainian population of the region.[[39]](#cite_note-rezumat-40)[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14) In addition to the suppression of the Ukrainian people, their language and culture, Ukrainian surnames were Rumanized, and the Ukrainian Orthodox Church was persecuted.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) In the 1930s an underground nationalist movement, which was led by Orest Zybachynsky and Denys Kvitkovsky, emerged in the region.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) The Romanian government suppressed it by staging two political trials in 1937.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

At the same time, Ukrainian enrollment at the [Cernăuți University](/wiki/Chernivtsi_University "Chernivtsi University") fell from 239 out of 1671, in 1914, to 155 out of 3,247, in 1933, while simultaneously Romanian enrollment there increased several times to 2,117 out of 3,247.[[40]](#cite_note-42) In part this was due to attempts to switch to Romanian as the primary language of university instruction, but chiefly to the fact that the university was one of only five in Romania, and was considered prestigious.

In the decade following 1928, as Romania tried to improve its relations with the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), Ukrainian culture was given some limited means to redevelop, though these gains were sharply reversed in 1938.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

According to the 1930 Romanian census, [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") made up 44.5% of the total population of Bukovina, and Ukrainians (including Hutsuls) 29.1%.[[41]](#cite_note-43) In the northern part of the region, however, Romanians made up only 32.6% of the population, with Ukrainians significantly outnumbering Romanians.

On 14 August 1938 Bukovina officially disappeared from the map, becoming a part of [Ținutul Suceava](/wiki/%C8%9Ainutul_Suceava "Ținutul Suceava"), one of [ten new administrative regions](/wiki/%C8%9Ainuturi "Ținuturi"). At the same time, Cernăuți, the third most populous town in Romania (after [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") and [Chișinău](/wiki/Chi%C8%99in%C4%83u "Chișinău")), which had been a mere county seat for the last 20 years, became again a (regional) capital. Also, Bukovinian regionalism continued under the new brand. During its first months of existence, Ținutul Suceava suffered far right ([Iron Guard](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard")) uproars, to which the regional governor [Gheorghe Alexianu](/wiki/Gheorghe_Alexianu "Gheorghe Alexianu") (the future governor of the [Transnistria Governorate](/wiki/Transnistria_Governorate "Transnistria Governorate")) reacted with nationalist and anti-Semitic measures. Alexianu was replaced by Gheorghe Flondor on 1 February 1939.

### Division of Bukovina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Division of Bukovina")]

See also: [Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Bucovina_division.svg/250px-Bucovina_division.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bucovina_division.svg)

Bukovina as divided in 1940: Soviet to the north, Romanian to the south.

As a result of the [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact"), the USSR demanded not only [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") but also the northern half of Bukovina and [Hertsa](/wiki/Hertsa_region "Hertsa region") regions from Romania on 26 June 1940 (Bukovina bordered [Eastern Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Central_Europe) "Galicia (Central Europe)"), which the USSR had annexed during the [Invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland")). Initially, the USSR wanted the whole of Bukovina.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), which was surprised by the Soviet claim to Bukovina,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] invoked the presence of ethnic Germans living in the region to protest the claim. As a result,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the USSR only demanded the northern, overwhelmingly Ukrainian part, arguing that it was a "reparation for the great loss produced to the Soviet Union and [Bassarabia](/wiki/Bassarabia "Bassarabia")'s population by twenty-two years of Romanian domination of [Bassarabia](/wiki/Bassarabia "Bassarabia")". Following the Soviet ultimatum, Romania ceded Northern Bukovina, which included Cernăuți, to the USSR on 28 June 1940. The withdrawal of the Romanian Army, authorities, and civilians was disastrous. Mobs attacked retreating soldiers and civilians, whereas a retreating unit [massacred Jewish soldiers and civilians in the town of Dorohoi](/wiki/Dorohoi_pogrom "Dorohoi pogrom"). The Red Army occupied [Cernăuți](/wiki/Cern%C4%83u%C8%9Bi_County "Cernăuți County") and [Storojineț](/wiki/Storojine%C8%9B_County "Storojineț County") counties, as well as parts of [Rădăuți](/wiki/R%C4%83d%C4%83u%C8%9Bi_County "Rădăuți County") and [Dorohoi](/wiki/Dorohoi_County "Dorohoi County") counties (the latter belonged to Ținutul Suceava, but not to Bukovina). The new Soviet-Romanian border was traced less than 20 kilometres (12 miles) north of [Putna Monastery](/wiki/Putna_Monastery "Putna Monastery"). Until 22 September 1940, when Ținutul Suceava was abolished, the spa town [Vatra Dornei](/wiki/Vatra_Dornei "Vatra Dornei") served as the capital of Ținutul Suceava.[[42]](#cite_note-44)

#### Second World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Second World War")]

Main article: [Romania in World War II](/wiki/Romania_in_World_War_II "Romania in World War II")

In 1940, [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast") (2⁄3 of which is Northern Bukovina) had a population of circa 805,000, out of which 47.5% were Ukrainians and 28.3% were Romanians, with Germans, Jews, Poles, Hungarians, and Russians comprising the rest.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The strong Ukrainian presence was the official motivation for the inclusion of the region into the [Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR") and not into the newly formed [Moldavian SSR](/wiki/Moldavian_SSR "Moldavian SSR"). Whether the region would have been included in the Moldavian SSR, if the commission presiding over the division had been led by someone other than the communist leader [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev"), remains a matter of debate among scholars.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In fact, some territories with a mostly Romanian population (e.g., [Hertsa region](/wiki/Hertsa_region "Hertsa region")) were allotted to the Ukrainian SSR.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/GUVERNAMANTUL_BUCOVINEI.png/250px-GUVERNAMANTUL_BUCOVINEI.png)](/wiki/File:GUVERNAMANTUL_BUCOVINEI.png)

Administrative map of the [Bukovina Governorate](/wiki/Bukovina_Governorate "Bukovina Governorate") as of May 1942

After the instauration of Soviet rule, under [NKVD](/wiki/NKVD "NKVD") orders, thousands of local families were deported to [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") during this period,[[43]](#cite_note-noinu-45) with 12,191 people targeted for deportation in a document dated 2 August 1940 (from all formerly Romanian regions included in the Ukrainian SSR),[[43]](#cite_note-noinu-45) while a December 1940 document listed 2,057 persons to be deported to Siberia.[[44]](#cite_note-46) The largest action took place on 13 June 1941, when about 13,000 people were deported to Siberia and Kazakhstan.[[45]](#cite_note-47) The total number of deportees from Soviet Moldova to Siberia in June 1941 and of people eligible for deportation who were not deported was 31,699, while 8,374 (mostly Romanians) were deported or eligible for deportation from the Chernivtsi oblast of Ukraine and 3,767 from the Izmail oblast of Ukraine (southern Bessarabia); the total was 43,840.[[46]](#cite_note-48) According to Bougai, 22,643 individuals from Soviet Moldova were deported in September 1941.[[47]](#cite_note-49) Only 1,136 of those deported from the Izmail oblast were still alive in Western Siberia, in the Tomsk region, in 1951, but others were sent to other places.[[48]](#cite_note-50) The number of deportees to the Soviet north and east from the [Hertsa raion](/wiki/Hertsa_Raion "Hertsa Raion") in its boundaries from early 2020 of the [Chernivtsi oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast") on June 13, 1941, was 1,373; 219 (15.95%) of them would later die in Siberia and Kazakhstan.[[49]](#cite_note-51) The number of inhabitants of the Chernivtsi Oblast who were deported to Siberia and Kazakhstan was 7,720 (2,279 families).[[50]](#cite_note-52) According to some sources, most of the deportees of June 1941 from the Chernivtsi oblast, who were of many ethnicities, did not return from the Soviet east.[[51]](#cite_note-53) However, the fragmentary, locality-by-locality, evidence indicates that most of the deportees from 1941 survived.[[52]](#cite_note-54) The majority of those targeted were ethnic native [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians"), but there were (to a lesser degree) representatives of other ethnicities, as well.[[53]](#cite_note-55) According to Dr. Avigdor Schachan, who wrote a book about the Transnistrian ghettos, and was himself brought up in the Bessarabian part of the present-day Chernivtsi Oblast of Ukraine, about 2,000 northern Bukovinian and 4,000 Bessarabian Jews were deported to the Soviet east.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

Until the repatriation convention[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] of 15 April 1941, NKVD troops killed hundreds of Romanian peasants of Northern Bukovina as they tried to cross the border into Romania to escape from Soviet authorities. This culminated on 7 February 1941 with the [Lunca massacre](/wiki/Lunca_massacre "Lunca massacre") and on 1 April 1941 with the [Fântâna Albă massacre](/wiki/F%C3%A2nt%C3%A2na_Alb%C4%83_massacre "Fântâna Albă massacre").

During Soviet Communist rule in Bukovina, "private property was nationalized; farms were partly collectivized; and education was Ukrainianized. At the same time all Ukrainian organizations were disbanded, and many publicly active Ukrainians were either killed or exiled." A significant part of Ukrainian intelligentsia fled to Romania and Germany in the beginning of the occupation.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) When the conflict between the Soviets and Nazi Germany broke out, and the Soviet troops began moving out of Bukovina, the Ukrainian locals attempted to establish their own government, but they were not able to stop the advancing Romanian army.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Heinrich_Himmler%2C_1900-1945_%28meeting_buconivian%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Heinrich_Himmler%2C_1900-1945_%28meeting_buconivian%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heinrich_Himmler,_1900-1945_(meeting_buconivian)_(cropped).jpg)

[Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler") handing naturalization papers to a repatriated Buconivian, [Breslau](/wiki/Breslau "Breslau"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") (now [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland")). c. 1941.

Almost the entire [German population of Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina_Germans "Bukovina Germans") was [coerced to resettle](/wiki/Heim_ins_Reich "Heim ins Reich") in 1940–1941 to the parts of Poland then occupied by Nazi Germany, during 15 September 1940 – 15 November 1940, after this area was occupied by the Soviet Union. About 45,000 ethnic Germans had left Northern Bukovina by November 1940.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

In the course of the [1941 attack on the Soviet Union](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa") by the [Axis forces](/wiki/Axis_forces "Axis forces"), the [Romanian Third Army](/wiki/Romanian_Third_Army "Romanian Third Army") led by General [Petre Dumitrescu](/wiki/Petre_Dumitrescu "Petre Dumitrescu") (operating in the north), and the [Fourth Romanian Army](/wiki/Fourth_Army_(Romania) "Fourth Army (Romania)") (operating in the south) regained Northern Bukovina, as well as [Hertsa](/wiki/Hertsa_region "Hertsa region"), and [Bassarabia](/wiki/Bassarabia "Bassarabia"), during June–July 1941. It was organized as part of the [Bukovina Governorate](/wiki/Bukovina_Governorate "Bukovina Governorate").

The Axis invasion of Northern Bukovina was catastrophic for its Jewish population, as conquering Romanian soldiers immediately began massacring its Jewish residents. Surviving Jews were forced into ghettoes to await deportation to work camps in Transnistria where 57,000 had arrived by 1941. One of the Romanian mayors of Cernăuți, [Traian Popovici](/wiki/Traian_Popovici "Traian Popovici"), managed to temporarily exempt from deportation 20,000 Jews living in the city between the fall of 1941 and the spring of 1942. Bukovina's remaining Jews were spared from certain death when it was retaken by Soviet forces in February 1944. In all, about half of Bukovina's entire Jewish population had perished.[[56]](#cite_note-58) In southern Bukovina (the territory that is currently a part of Romania), in the counties of Campulung, Suceava and Radauti, there were 18,893 Jews according to the September 1, 1941 census; after the deportations to Transnistria later in the year, there were only 179 Jews in 1942.[[57]](#cite_note-59) If one excludes the orphans, about 12,000 of the southern Bukovinian Jewish deportees survived the deportations to Transnistria.[[58]](#cite_note-60) According to the Israeli scholar Gali Mir-Tibon 2018 study, over 70% of the Jews of southern Bukovina deported to Transnistria survived, with the orphans included in that figure.[[59]](#cite_note-61) According to Gali Mir-Tibon, most of the Jews deported from the city of Chernivtsi, and northern Bukovina in general, to Transnistria did not survive.[[60]](#cite_note-62) About 60% of the deportees to Transnistria from the city of Chernivtsi perished according to the Jewish Virtual Library.[[61]](#cite_note-63) After the war and the return of the Soviets, most of the Jewish survivors from Northern Bukovina fled to Romania (and later settled in Israel).[[62]](#cite_note-64) For more information on the Holocaust in [Transnistria](/wiki/Transnistria "Transnistria"), including on the fate of the Jewish deportees from Bukovina, see [History of the Jews in Transnistria](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Transnistria "History of the Jews in Transnistria").

According to Dr. Avigdor Schachan, who wrote a book about the Transnistrian ghettos, and was himself brought up in the Bessarabian part of the present-day Chernivtsi Oblast of Ukraine, about 2,000 northern Bukovinian and 4,000 Bessarabian Jews were deported to the Soviet east in June 1941.[[63]](#cite_note-65) About half of the Jews deported from Bessarabia to the Soviet east survived and returned to Bessarabia according to a source mentioned by Jean Ancel (Matathias Carp), the specialist on the Holocaust in Romania and Transnistria, whereas the rest did not return.[[64]](#cite_note-66) For more information on the history of the Jews of Bukovina, including during the Holocaust, see [History of the Jews in Bukovina](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Bukovina "History of the Jews in Bukovina").

On October 11, 1942, the (Soviet) State Committee on Defense decided to extend the decrees on "the mobilization of the NKVD labour columns, German men, able to work, 17-50 years old - to the persons of other nations, being in war with USSR-Romanians, Hungarians, Italians, Finns."; the order was signed by Stalin.[[65]](#cite_note-67) Authors Ion Popescu and Constantin Ungureanu, and their source, Aurel Popovici, claim that in May 1944, in the village of [Molodiia](/wiki/Molodiia "Molodiia") and some other northern Bukovinian localities, those men who declared a "Moldovan" nationality were incorporated into the Soviet army, while those who declared a "Romanian" nationality were sent to the work camps in the area of Lake Onega, where most of them died.[[66]](#cite_note-68) In March 1945, 3,967 Romanian citizens from Ukraine (excluding Jews), mostly from the Chernivtsi Oblast, were sent to the Soviet east.[[67]](#cite_note-69) Popescu and Ungureanu, and their source, Dumitru Covalciuc, claim that the Soviet era dominance of the "Moldovan" identity in parts of northern Bukovina was due to the fact that the inhabitants of the Chernivtsi and Sadagura rural raions, and of the Bukovinian part of the [Novoselytsia raion](/wiki/Novoselytsia_Raion "Novoselytsia Raion"), were pressured in 1944 to adopt a "Moldovan" national/ethnic identity.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Bucovina_Romania.svg/250px-Bucovina_Romania.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bucovina_Romania.svg)

Southern Bukovina within Romania

In 1944 the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") drove the [Axis forces](/wiki/Axis_forces "Axis forces") out and re-established Soviet control over the territory. Romania was forced to formally cede the northern part of Bukovina to the [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR") by the 1947 [Paris peace treaty](/wiki/Paris_peace_treaty "Paris peace treaty"). The territory became part of the [Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR") as [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast") ([province](/wiki/Oblast "Oblast")). While during the war the Soviet government killed or forced in exile a considerable number of [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians"),[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) after the war the same government deported or killed about 41,000 [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians").[[69]](#cite_note-71) As a result of killings and mass deportations, entire villages, mostly inhabited by Romanians,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] were abandoned (Albovat, Frunza, I.G.Duca, Buci—completely erased, Prisaca, Tanteni and Vicov—destroyed to a large extent).[[70]](#cite_note-Tara-72) Men of military age (and sometimes above), both Ukrainians and Romanians, were conscripted into the Soviet Army. That did not protect them, however, from being arrested and deported for being "anti-Soviet elements".

As a reaction, partisan groups (composed of both Romanians and Ukrainians) began to operate against the Soviets in the woods around [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi"), Crasna and [Codrii Cosminului](/w/index.php?title=Codrii_Cosminului&action=edit&redlink=1 "Codrii Cosminului (page does not exist)").[[71]](#cite_note-73) In Crasna (in the former [Storozhynets](/wiki/Storozhynets "Storozhynets") county) villagers attacked Soviet soldiers who were sent to "temporarily resettle" them, since they feared deportation. This resulted in dead and wounded among the villagers, who had no firearms.

Spring 1945 saw the formation of transports of Polish repatriates who (voluntarily or by coercion) had decided to leave. Between March 1945 and July 1946, 10,490 inhabitants left Northern Bukovina for Poland, including 8,140 Poles, 2,041 Jews and 309 of other nationalities. Most of them settled in [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia"), near the towns: [Bolesławiec](/wiki/Boles%C5%82awiec "Bolesławiec"), [Dzierżoniów](/wiki/Dzier%C5%BConi%C3%B3w "Dzierżoniów"), [Gubin](/wiki/Gubin,_Poland "Gubin, Poland"), [Lubań Śląski](/wiki/Luba%C5%84_%C5%9Al%C4%85ski "Lubań Śląski"), [Lwówek Śląski](/wiki/Lw%C3%B3wek_%C5%9Al%C4%85ski "Lwówek Śląski"), [Nowa Sól](/wiki/Nowa_S%C3%B3l "Nowa Sól"), [Oława](/wiki/O%C5%82awa "Oława"), [Prudnik](/wiki/Prudnik "Prudnik"), [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"), [Zielona Góra](/wiki/Zielona_G%C3%B3ra "Zielona Góra"), [Żagań](/wiki/%C5%BBaga%C5%84 "Żagań"), [Żary](/wiki/%C5%BBary "Żary").[[72]](#cite_note-74)

Overall, between 1930 (last Romanian census) and 1959 (first Soviet census), the population of Northern Bukovina decreased by 31,521 people. According to official data from those two censuses, the Romanian population had decreased by 75,752 people, and the Jewish population by 46,632, while the Ukrainian and Russian populations increased by 135,161 and 4,322 people, respectively.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After 1944, the human and economic connections between the northern (Soviet) and southern (Romanian) parts of Bukovina were severed. Today, the historically Ukrainian northern part is the nucleus of the Ukrainian [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast"), while the southern part is part of Romania, though there are minorities of Ukrainians and Romanians in Romanian Bukovina and Ukrainian Bukovina respectively. [Ukrainians are still a recognized minority in Romania](/wiki/Ukrainians_of_Romania "Ukrainians of Romania"), and have one seat reserved in the Romanian Chamber of Deputies.

In Romania, 28 November is a holiday observed as [Bukovina Day](/wiki/Bukovina_Day "Bukovina Day").[[73]](#cite_note-75) A popular Romanian-language song about the region is "[Cântă cucu-n Bucovina](/wiki/C%C3%A2nt%C4%83_cucu-n_Bucovina "Cântă cucu-n Bucovina")" ("Sings the Cuckoo in Bukovina").[[74]](#cite_note-76)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Geography")]

Bukovina proper has an area of 10,442 km2 (4,032 sq mi). The territory of Romanian (or Southern) Bukovina is located in [northeastern Romania](/wiki/Historical_regions_of_Romania "Historical regions of Romania") and it is part of the [Suceava County](/wiki/Suceava_County "Suceava County") (plus three localities in [Botoșani County](/wiki/Boto%C8%99ani_County "Botoșani County")), whereas Ukrainian (or Northern) Bukovina is located in [western Ukraine](/wiki/Ukrainian_historical_regions "Ukrainian historical regions") and it is part of the [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast").

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Population")]

### Historical population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Historical population")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Bucov.png/250px-Bucov.png)](/wiki/File:Bucov.png)

Demographic composition of Bukovina in 1930

The region was occupied by several now extinct peoples. After which it was settled by both Romanians (Moldavians) and Ukrainians (Ruthenians)[[75]](#cite_note-77) with the [Antes](/wiki/Antes_(people) "Antes (people)") controlling a large area that included Bukovina by the 6th century. Later, the region was part of Kievan Rus', and later still of the [Kingdom of Galicia–Volhynia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia%E2%80%93Volhynia "Kingdom of Galicia–Volhynia"). During this period it reinforced its ties to other Ukrainian lands, with many Bukovinian natives studying in Lviv and Kyiv, and the Orthodox Bukovinian Church flourishing in the region. After passing to Hungary in the 14th century, the Hungarian king appointed Dragoș as his deputy forming the [principality of Moldavia](/wiki/Principality_of_Moldavia "Principality of Moldavia"), following the revolt of [Bogdan the Founder](/wiki/Bogdan_the_Founder "Bogdan the Founder") against the Kingdom of Hungary, Bukovina became an integral part of the principality of Moldavia. Suceava, in the south of the territory, was the capital of Moldavia from the late 14th to the mid-16th century. The only data we have about the ethnic composition of Bukovina are the Austrian censuses starting from the 1770s. The Austrians hindered both Romanian and Ukrainian nationalisms. On the other hand, they favored the migration in Bukovina of Ukrainians from Galicia as well as Romanians from Transylvania and Maramureș.

According to the 1775 Austrian census, the province had a total population of 86,000 (this included 56 villages which were returned to Moldavia one year later). The census only recorded social status and some ethno-religious groups (Jews, [Armenians](/wiki/Armenians "Armenians"), [Roma](/wiki/Roma_people "Roma people"), and German colonists). Historian [Ion Nistor](/wiki/Ion_Nistor "Ion Nistor") estimated that the 1774 population consisted of 52,750 Romanians (also called Moldavians) (73.5%), 15,000 [Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians") and [Hutsuls](/wiki/Hutsuls "Hutsuls") (20.9%) (of whom 6,000 were Hutsuls, and 9,000 were Ruthenian immigrants from [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Eastern_Europe) "Galicia (Eastern Europe)") and [Podolia](/wiki/Podolia "Podolia") settled in Moldavia around 1766), and 4,000 others who "use the Romanian language in conversation" (5.6%), consisting of Armenians, Jews and Roma.[[76]](#cite_note-78) [Keith Hitchins](/wiki/Keith_Hitchins "Keith Hitchins") on the other hand, estimated that in 1774 Bukovina's population consisted of 64,000 Romanians, 8,000 Ukrainians and 3,000 Germans, Jews, and Poles.[[27]](#cite_note-roman1774-28) According to Alecu Hurmuzaki, by 1848, out of a population of 377,581 people, 209,293 or 55.4% of the population was Romanian. At the same time, the Ukrainian population rose to 108,907 and the Jewish population surged from 526 in 1774, to 11,600 in 1848.[[27]](#cite_note-roman1774-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Biserica_Adormirea_Maicii_Domnului_din_Dornesti3.jpg/250px-Biserica_Adormirea_Maicii_Domnului_din_Dornesti3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biserica_Adormirea_Maicii_Domnului_din_Dornesti3.jpg)

[Dornești](/wiki/Dorne%C8%99ti "Dornești") (German: *Kriegsdorf*, [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"): *Hadikfalva*), [Suceava County](/wiki/Suceava_County "Suceava County"), an example of a former mixed German-Hungarian rural settlement in Bukovina.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/HerrenGasse_761.jpg/250px-HerrenGasse_761.jpg)](/wiki/File:HerrenGasse_761.jpg)

The Polish House in [Cernăuți](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi") ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *Czerniowce*, German: *Czernowitz*)

In 2011, an anthropological analysis of the Russian census of the population of Moldavia in 1774 asserted a population of 68,700 people in 1774, out of which 40,920 (59.6%) Romanians, 22,810 Ruthenians and Hutsuls (33.2%), and 7.2% Jews, Roma, and Armenians.[[28]](#cite_note-ceeol-29)

Based on the above anthropological estimate for 1774 as well as subsequent official censuses, the ethnic composition of Bukovina changed in the years after 1775 when the Austrian Empire occupied the region.[[10]](#cite_note-MIac-11) The population of Bukovina increased steadily, primarily through immigration, which Austrian authorities encouraged to develop the economy.[[77]](#cite_note-wesen-79) Indeed, the migrants entering the region came from Ukrainian Galicia, as well as from Romanian Transylvania and Moldavia.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12) Another Austrian official report from 1783, referring to the villages between the Dniester and the Prut, indicated Ruthenian-speaking immigrants from Poland constituting a majority, with only a quarter of the population speaking Moldavian. The same report indicated that Moldavians constituted the majority in the area of Suceava.[[78]](#cite_note-80) H.F. Müller gives the 1840 population used for purposes of military conscription as 339,669.[[79]](#cite_note-81)

In 1843 the [Ruthenian language](/wiki/Ruthenian_language "Ruthenian language") was recognized, along with the [Romanian language](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), as 'the language of the people and of the Church in Bukovina'.[[80]](#cite_note-jew-82)

During the 19th century, as mentioned, the Austrian Empire policies encouraged the influx of migrants coming from Transylvania, Moldavia, Galicia and the heartland of Austria and Germany, with Germans, Poles, Jews, Hungarians, Romanians, and Ukrainians settling in the region.[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)[[80]](#cite_note-jew-82) Official censuses in the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") (later [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary")) did not record ethnolinguistic data until 1850–1851. The 1857 and 1869 censuses omitted ethnic or language-related questions. 'Familiar language spoken' was not recorded again until 1880.

The Austrian census of 1850–1851, which recorded data regarding languages spoken, shows 48.50% [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") and 38.07% [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians").[[81]](#cite_note-83) Subsequent Austrian censuses between 1880 and 1910 reveal a Romanian population stabilizing around 33% and a Ukrainian population around 40%. From 1774 to 1910, the percentage of Ukrainians increased, meanwhile the one of Romanians decreased.[[10]](#cite_note-MIac-11)

According to the [1930 Romanian census](/wiki/Demographic_history_of_Romania "Demographic history of Romania"), Bukovina had a population of 853,009.[[82]](#cite_note-Liv-84) Romanians made up 44.5% of the population, while 27.7% were Ukrainians/Ruthenians (plus 1.5% Hutsuls), 10.8% Jews, 8.9% Germans, 3.6% Poles, and 3.0% others or undeclared.[[83]](#cite_note-85)
