### Current population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bukovina&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Current population")]

The present demographic situation in Bukovina hardly resembles that of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"). The northern (Ukrainian) and southern (Romanian) parts became significantly dominated by their Ukrainian and Romanian majorities, respectively, with the representation of other ethnic groups being decreased significantly.

According to the data of the [2001 Ukrainian census](/wiki/2001_Ukrainian_census "2001 Ukrainian census"),[[90]](#cite_note-92) the [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") represent about 75% (689,100) of the population of [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast"), which is the closest, although not an exact, approximation of the territory of the historic Northern Bukovina. The census also identified a fall in the Romanian and [Moldovan](/wiki/Moldovans "Moldovans") populations to 12.5% (114,600) and 7.3% (67,200), respectively. [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians") are the next largest ethnic group with 4.1%, while [Poles](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people"), [Belarusians](/wiki/Belarusians "Belarusians"), and Jews comprise the rest 1.2%. The languages of the population closely reflect the ethnic composition, with over 90% within each of the major ethnic groups declaring their national language as the mother tongue ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"), [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"), and Russian, respectively).

The fact that [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") and [Moldovans](/wiki/Moldovans "Moldovans"), a [self-declared](/wiki/Self-declared "Self-declared") majority in some regions, were presented as separate categories in the census results, has been criticized in Romania, where there are complains that this artificial Soviet-era practice results in the Romanian population being undercounted, as being divided between [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") and [Moldovans](/wiki/Moldovans "Moldovans"). The Romanian minority of Ukraine also claims to represent a 500,000-strong community.[[91]](#cite_note-93)[[92]](#cite_note-94)[[93]](#cite_note-95)

The Romanians mostly inhabit the southern part of the Chernivtsi region, having been the majority in former [Hertsa Raion](/wiki/Hertsa_Raion "Hertsa Raion") and forming a majority together with Moldovans in former [Hlyboka Raion](/wiki/Hlyboka_Raion "Hlyboka Raion"). According to the [Ukraine Census (2001)](/wiki/Ukraine_Census_(2001) "Ukraine Census (2001)"), the 72,676 residents of the raion reported themselves as following: Ukrainians: 34,025 (46.82%), Romanians: 32,923 (45.3%), Moldovans: 4,425 (6.09%), Russians: 877 (1.21%), and others: 426 (0.59%).[[94]](#cite_note-96) Hlyboka raion, within its boundaries at that time, had 72,676 inhabitants in 2001, including 52.56% Ukrainian-speakers, 45.97% Romanian-speakers, and 1.15% Russian-speakers.[[95]](#cite_note-97)[[96]](#cite_note-98) In 2001, the population of [Hertsa Raion](/wiki/Hertsa_Raion "Hertsa Raion") was 32,316, of which 29,554 or 91.45% identified themselves as [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians"), 1,616 or 5.0% as [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians"), and 756 or 2.34% as [Moldovans](/wiki/Moldovans "Moldovans") (out of which 511 self-identified their language as Moldovan and 237 as Romanian), 0.9% as [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians"), and 0.3% as being of other ethnicities (*see: [Ukrainian Census, 2001](/wiki/Ukrainian_Census_(2001) "Ukrainian Census (2001)")*).[[97]](#cite_note-99)[[98]](#cite_note-100) Self-declared Moldovans were the majority in the overwhelmingly Bessarabian rather than Bukovinian [Novoselytsia Raion](/wiki/Novoselytsia_Raion "Novoselytsia Raion"). In [Novoselytsia Raion](/wiki/Novoselytsia_Raion "Novoselytsia Raion"), among the 50,329 self-identified Moldovans (57.54%), 47,585 (54.54%) self-identified their language as Moldovan and 2,264 as Romanian (2.6%) according to the Ukrainian census of 2001; there were also 29,703 self-identified Ukrainians (35.05%), 5,904 Romanians (6.77%), 1,235 Russians (1.42%), and 290 others (0.29%).[[99]](#cite_note-101)[[100]](#cite_note-102) Novoselytsia raion, within its boundaries at that time, had 87,241 inhabitants in 2001, including 34.08% Ukrainian-speakers, 64% Romanian-speakers, and 1.78% Russian-speakers.[[101]](#cite_note-103)[[102]](#cite_note-104) In the other eight districts and the city of [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi"), [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") were the majority.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, after the 2020 administrative reform in Ukraine, all these districts were abolished, and most of the areas merged into [Chernivtsi Raion](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Raion,_Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Raion, Chernivtsi Oblast"), where Romanians are not in majority anymore.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Muzeul_Arta_Lemnului_C%C3%A2mpulung_Moldovenesc_2022.jpg/250px-Muzeul_Arta_Lemnului_C%C3%A2mpulung_Moldovenesc_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muzeul_Arta_Lemnului_C%C3%A2mpulung_Moldovenesc_2022.jpg)

The renovated Wood Art Museum situated in [Câmpulung Moldovenesc](/wiki/C%C3%A2mpulung_Moldovenesc "Câmpulung Moldovenesc") (German: *Kimpolung*).

The southern, or Romanian Bukovina reportedly has a significant Romanian majority (94.8%) according to Romanian sources, the largest minority group being the [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") (1.9%) and Ukrainians, who make up 0.9% of the population (2011 census). Other minor ethnic groups include [Lipovans](/wiki/Lipovans "Lipovans"), [Poles](/wiki/Poles_in_Romania "Poles in Romania") (in [Cacica](/wiki/Cacica "Cacica"), [Mănăstirea Humorului](/wiki/M%C4%83n%C4%83stirea_Humorului "Mănăstirea Humorului"), [Mușenița](/wiki/Mu%C8%99eni%C8%9Ba "Mușenița"), [Moara](/wiki/Moara,_Suceava "Moara, Suceava"), and [Păltinoasa](/wiki/P%C4%83ltinoasa "Păltinoasa")), [Zipser Germans](/wiki/Zipser_Germans "Zipser Germans") (in [Cârlibaba](/wiki/C%C3%A2rlibaba "Cârlibaba") and [Iacobeni](/wiki/Iacobeni,_Suceava "Iacobeni, Suceava")) and [Bukovina Germans](/wiki/Bukovina_Germans "Bukovina Germans") (in [Suceava](/wiki/Suceava "Suceava"), [Rădăuți](/wiki/R%C4%83d%C4%83u%C8%9Bi "Rădăuți"), and [Câmpulung Moldovenesc](/wiki/C%C3%A2mpulung_Moldovenesc "Câmpulung Moldovenesc")), as well as [Slovaks](/wiki/Slovaks_of_Romania "Slovaks of Romania") and [Jews](/wiki/Jews_in_Romania "Jews in Romania") (almost exclusively in [Suceava](/wiki/Suceava "Suceava"), [Rădăuți](/wiki/R%C4%83d%C4%83u%C8%9Bi "Rădăuți") and [Siret](/wiki/Siret "Siret")).

Concerns have been raised about the way census are handled in Romania.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[neutrality](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view") is [disputed](/wiki/Talk:Bukovina "Talk:Bukovina")*] For example, according to the 2011 Romanian census, [Ukrainians of Romania](/wiki/Ukrainians_of_Romania "Ukrainians of Romania") number 51,703 people, making up 0.3% of the total population.[[103]](#cite_note-insse3-105) However, Ukrainian nationalists[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] of the 1990s claimed the region had 110,000 Ukrainians.[[104]](#cite_note-wilson-106)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The Ukrainian descendants of the [Zaporozhian Cossacks](/wiki/Zaporozhian_Cossacks "Zaporozhian Cossacks") who fled Russian rule in the 18th century, living in the [Dobruja](/wiki/Dobruja "Dobruja") region of the [Danube Delta](/wiki/Danube_Delta "Danube Delta"), also complained similar practices. In 1992, their descendants numbered four thousand people according to official Romanian statistics.[[105]](#cite_note-anri-107) However, the local community claims to number 20,000, five times the number stated by Romanian authorities.[[106]](#cite_note-uur-108) Rumanization, with the closure of schools and suppression of the language, happened in all areas in present-day Romania where the Ukrainians live or lived. The very term "Ukrainians" was prohibited from the official usage and some Romanians of disputable Ukrainian ethnicity were rather called the "citizens of Romania who forgot their native language" and were forced to change their last names to Romanian-sounding ones.[[107]](#cite_note-Derh-109) In Bukovina, the practice of Romanization dates to much earlier than the 20th century. Since Louis of Hungary appointed Dragoș, Voivode of Moldavia as his deputy, there was an introduction of Romanians in Bukovina, and a process of Romanization that intensified in the 1560s.[[13]](#cite_note-scare-14)[[11]](#cite_note-encyclo-12)

Places such as the etymologically Ukrainian [Breaza](/wiki/Breaza,_Suceava "Breaza, Suceava") and [Moldovița](/wiki/Moldovi%C8%9Ba "Moldovița") (whose name in German is *Russ Moldawitza*, and used to be *Ruska Moldavyda* in Ukrainian), [Șerbăuți](/wiki/%C8%98erb%C4%83u%C8%9Bi "Șerbăuți") and [Siret](/wiki/Siret "Siret") used to have an overwhelming Ukrainian majority. In some places in southern Bukovina, such as [Balkivtsi](/wiki/B%C4%83lc%C4%83u%C8%9Bi,_Suceava "Bălcăuți, Suceava") (Romanian: *Bălcăuți*), [Izvoarele Sucevei](/wiki/Izvoarele_Sucevei "Izvoarele Sucevei"), [Ulma](/wiki/Ulma,_Suceava "Ulma, Suceava") and [Negostina](/wiki/Negostina_(town) "Negostina (town)"), Ukrainian majority is still reported in Romanian census. On other hand in North Bukovina the Romanians used to be the biggest ethnic group in the city of [Chernivtsi](/wiki/Chernivtsi "Chernivtsi"), as well as in the towns of [Hlyboka](/wiki/Hlyboka "Hlyboka") and [Storozhynets](/wiki/Storozhynets "Storozhynets"), and still are in [Boiany](/wiki/Boiany "Boiany") and [Krasnoilsk](/wiki/Krasnoilsk "Krasnoilsk").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
